dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for abdominal pain. 57-year-old female, 91 kg, at 312 Redacted in [CITY].
Hello, we're EMS. You can call us Alex and Sam. What can we help you with today?
The patient reported 10/10 abdominal pain, mainly in the left and right lower quadrants. The pain started worsening months ago and became unbearable last night.
She mentioned guarding her abdomen and has two bandaged surgical sites under her belly button and in the RLQ.
She is A&Ox4 and walking with assistance because of the pain, but she doesn't have obvious signs of trauma.
She's experiencing nausea, fatigue, and some mild lethargy. There was no vomiting observed.
Swelling is noted in both ankles, but she denies losing consciousness or having new injuries.
She was conscious, watching our actions, and making eye contact during the assessment.
She gave full answers to all our questions and described her pain well.
She followed our directions and moved her legs and arms when asked.
She remained Alert with no signs of confusion during the entire interaction.
Her airway seemed unobstructed at first glance.
She was seated on the bed, and that posture did not appear to cause any breathing difficulties.
Her breathing was 18 breaths per minute and regular in depth and rhythm.
No signs of cyanosis were found.
Her effort to breathe didn't appear labored.
Her initial pulse was 84 and strong, but she described being less active.
No rapid heart rate or hypotension was seen during the first check.
Her first blood pressure reading was 108/75, with a consistent pulse rate.
The patient could not remember when she last ate or drank because of the discomfort she felt.
She first noticed persistent pain around her surgical sites about four months ago.
She has a known history of non-insulin-dependent diabetes, according to her records.
She mentions a medical condition of past hypotension and currently uses Clonazepam for anxiety.
She is taking several medications including Ventolin, Dyclonine, Cymbalta, and Klonopin as reported in her record.
She had multiple surgeries for hernia repair in the past and now has chronic pain at the surgical sites.
She also has mild asthma, which is managed with Ventolin inhalers.
She mentions a history of histrionic personality disorder and another unspecified personality disorder as listed in her past medical history.
First set of vitals: 84 beats per minute for pulse, 18 for respiratory rate.
Her breathing remained steady at 18 breaths per minute.
Her initial blood pressure was 108/75, with a consistent pulse rate.
She had 96% oxygen saturation when not on supplemental oxygen.
The blood sugar device did not read a value and was listed as 0 in our report.
The first EKG showed normal cardiac rhythm with no clear life-threatening findings.
A 12-lead EKG was performed and showed a non-specific T wave abnormality.
We attempted to start an IV in her extremity veins but were unsuccessful after two tries.
Follow-up vital signs showed pulse 75 and blood pressure 104/68.
She remained A&Ox4 with the same pain level at 10/10, and systolic pressure later dropped to 93/53.
Oxygen saturation improved to 98%, and she remained stable otherwise during transport.
We saw no signs of bruising, rashes, or open wounds elsewhere on her body.
Her non-surgical skin could not be fully evaluated due to bandage coverage in the LLQ and RLQ.
No unusual skin lesions or bites were found.
Her belly was smooth and not rigid, but mildly swollen around the middle.
Her pupils appeared normal and both were reactive to light.
No swollen glands in her neck were noted.
She remained alert and focused the whole time with good memory and awareness.
No unusual sounds were reported in her breathing or heart.
Her breath and heart sounds sounded normal for now.
She had a normal pulse in the central and peripheral regions.
When we touched her belly, she showed some tenderness but no major rigidity.
In the LLQ, there's a firm area that hurts more on direct palpation.
We found stitches and open marks covered by bandages in the RLQ and under her navel.
There was some mild edema in both ankles.
She moved her arms and legs with symmetrical motor function and sensation.
We carefully moved her to the stretcher and fastened it using all available straps.
We positioned her in a semi-Fowler's posture to improve comfort during transport.
She remained A&Ox4 with stable vitals throughout transport.
We selected [COMPANY_NAME] Emergency Department in [CITY] as the destination facility.
The patient's condition and vital signs remained unchanged during the transport.
The patient was managed under the medical - abdominal pain Protocol (3 - 2).
The condition of the patient did not change during transport, and we transferred care to the hospital staff.
